common_name: Western Wild Ginger (British Columbia Wild Ginger)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; western wild ginger forms slowly expanding mats by horizontal rhizomes under conifers, oaks, and maples in moist Pacific Northwest forests. Spreads more aggressively than eastern wild ginger (A. canadense).

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; western wild ginger is the native groundcover wild ginger of Pacific Northwest moist forests, producing large, glossy, dark evergreen, heart-shaped leaves and the characteristic small, brownish-maroon, three-pointed, urn-shaped flowers hidden at the base of the plant and pollinated by ground beetles and ants. The leaves have a pleasant ginger scent when crushed. Western wild ginger spreads more vigorously than eastern wild ginger by horizontal rhizomes, eventually forming dense, weed-suppressing mats under conifers and deciduous trees. An exceptional shade groundcover for the Pacific Northwest, competing effectively with English ivy and other invasives while providing excellent, low-maintenance native coverage. The rhizomes have been used as a ginger substitute by Pacific Northwest indigenous peoples, though in small quantities only — large amounts of Asarum species may be toxic. Evergreen foliage persists through Pacific Northwest winters.

common_name: Virginia Wild Ginger (Little Brown Jug Ginger)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; Virginia wild ginger is an evergreen woodland groundcover of Appalachian and Piedmont deciduous forests that slowly spreads by rhizomes to form low-growing patches.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; Virginia wild ginger is a native evergreen groundcover of Appalachian and Piedmont forests, closely related to the little-brown-jug (Hexastylis arifolia) and sometimes confused with it. The heart-shaped to arrowhead-shaped, leathery, dark evergreen leaves are ornamental year-round, providing low, dense coverage under deciduous trees. The odd, reddish-brown, jug-shaped flowers are hidden under the leaves at ground level, pollinated by ground beetles and ants. Like all Hexastylis species, it spreads slowly by rhizomes and self-seeds modestly to naturalize in appropriate shade conditions. Distinguished from H. arifolia by its broader distribution extending into Virginia and Maryland and subtle leaf differences. More cold-hardy than H. arifolia. An excellent, low-maintenance evergreen groundcover for Appalachian native woodland gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade to partial shade; Appalachian wild ginger is an evergreen shade groundcover of mountain and Piedmont forest understories of the central Appalachians, slowly spreading by rhizomes to form dense, low patches.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; Appalachian wild ginger is a variable-leaved native evergreen groundcover of the central and southern Appalachians, one of several closely related Hexastylis species with overlapping ranges in the mountain and foothill forests of the Southeast. The heterophylla species name (variable-leaved) refers to the tendency to produce leaves of varying shapes on the same plant or population. Like all Hexastylis, it produces the characteristic leathery, heart-shaped, dark evergreen leaves and the odd, urn-shaped, brownish-maroon flowers at ground level in spring, pollinated by ground beetles. A low, slow-spreading, extremely shade-tolerant groundcover that is virtually maintenance-free once established in appropriate forest conditions. Best combined with other evergreen groundcovers like wild ginger, green-and-gold (Chrysogonum), and Christmas fern for a natural mountain forest floor planting.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, lean, sandy or silty soil in full sun to partial shade; fringed gentian is a biennial that forms a rosette in its first year and blooms in late summer of its second. It requires moist, lean, calcareous or neutral soil and does not tolerate competition — it thrives in disturbed seep margins, wet meadow edges, and streambanks.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, lean, sandy or silty soil in full sun to partial shade; fringed gentian is one of the most beautiful and coveted native wildflowers of northeastern North America, producing brilliant violet-blue, vase-shaped flowers with dramatically fringed petal margins in late summer and fall — the fringed edges of each petal lobe are deeply cut into hundreds of fine filaments, creating an extraordinarily ornate, lacey appearance unique among North American wildflowers. The plant is notoriously difficult to establish in gardens, requiring very specific conditions — moist, open, lean, near-neutral soils free of competition, such as seep margins, calcareous stream banks, and disturbed wet meadows. Once established in appropriate conditions it self-sows to maintain a colony. John Muir rhapsodized about encountering it in Wisconsin prairies. A specialist plant for wet, calcareous, open sites — immensely rewarding when conditions are right.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moist, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wild cucumber is a vigorous annual vine that grows naturally along stream banks, woodland edges, and roadsides, climbing rapidly by tendrils over shrubs and small trees. Self-sows freely in moist conditions.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in moist, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wild cucumber is a vigorous native annual vine of moist woodland edges, streambanks, and disturbed riparian areas across most of North America, climbing rapidly by branching tendrils over shrubs, fences, and small trees to produce a dense, large-leaved screen in a single season. The large, maple-like leaves create excellent temporary screening. Small, white, star-shaped male flowers in large, fluffy panicles are mildly fragrant and attractive to many small native bees. The distinctive, spiny, oval, cucumber-like fruits (about the size of a golf ball) are ornamental and give the plant both its common names — the inflated, papery fruits are balsam-apple shaped and resemble tiny cucumbers covered in soft spines. The fruits split at maturity to release 4 large, flat seeds. An excellent, fast-growing native annual for covering trellises, fences, or unsightly structures in partially shaded, moist locations.
